{{ block title }} Part Two {{ endblock }} {{ block content }}

In Part Two of the experiment you will make decisions as the Seller, choosing {{ if single == 1 }} an amount of widgets {{ else }} a menu of widgets {{ endif }} to offer to the Buyer. In Part One there was some randomness in your round payoff as the Seller, affected by the ideal quantity drawn, and the particular Buyer participant you were matched to. In contrast, in Part Two we will remove this randomness, paying you a certain amount for any particular choice you might make. This certain amount of money on offer is the average expected payment from your choice over the {{ if single == 1 }} offer {{ else }} menu {{ endif }}.

To generate this payment amount, instead of being matched to another participant in this room, you will be matched to a large number of Robot Buyers, and paid the average Seller payment across those matches. Each Robot Buyer has been trained to reflect a best guess for the choices across [56] different Buyer participants that completed earlier sessions of this experiment, making choices in an identical Part One. Because of this training, for any {{ if single == 1 }} offer {{ else }} menu {{ endif }} you select, and any ideal quantity drawn from the urn, the Robot Buyers' decisions reflect our best guess over the distribution of decisions you could expect playing against each of the [56] prior participants.

For any {{ if single == 1 }} offer {{ else }} menu {{ endif }} you select in the Part Two decisions, you can think of your payment as being calculated by averaging the Seller's payoff across millions of different Robot Buyers. Each separate Robot Buyer represents a fair draw over both an ideal quantity from the given urn and and of the likely choices from of one of the [56] prior participants.

Because the payment rule averages across a large number of Robot Buyers, there is no longer any randomness in the payment for any specific {{ if single == 1 }} offer {{ else }} menu {{ endif }} you select as the Seller. Any choice you make will lead to a fixed additional payment. where that payment reflects our best guess on the average outcome for the Seller payment across the [56] prior participants and the 20 different balls that could be drawn from the urn to determine the Buyer’s ideal quantity.

Given this rule, your role in Part Two is to choose whichever {{ if single == 1 }} offer {{ else }} menu {{ endif }} you think will generate the highest average payment for the Seller. You will make a separate decision over a {{ if single == 1 }} offer to make {{ else }} menu to offer {{ endif }} for each of the three separate urns (Low, Middle, and High), where the different urns affect the chances for each Robot Buyer's ideal quantity.

{{ if test == 1 }} (Next button for testing purposes.)
{{ endif }} {{ endblock }}
